title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Nerve -- Surgical Considerations
content: An increase in the number of second molar implants has also increased the risk of injury to the nerve [3] . However, the lingual nerve most commonly gets damaged during inferior alveolar nerve block injections and third molar tooth removals [3] [10] . Reports indicate that damage to this nerve occurs during the removal of the third molar between 0 and 23% of the time [11] [12] . The wide variation in reported frequencies of this injury may be in part due to the variations seen in the course of the lingual nerve. Potential mechanisms for preventing lingual nerve injury during third molar surgery include avoiding lingual flap elevation and conducting tooth sectioning [8] [13] .

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Nerve -- Other Issues
content: The lingual nerve is more likely to be damaged during the administration of the inferior alveolar nerve block than the inferior alveolar nerve. Even though neural injuries are rare, it is imperative that dental professionals have deep knowledge of the anatomy of the lingual nerve and an understanding of the IAN block technique to avoid complications.

title: Local and Regional Anesthesia in Ophthalmology and Ocular Trauma -- Technique or Treatment -- Facial Nerve Blocks
content: Either form of this technique leads to akinesia of the facial/temporal and zygomatic branches of the facial nerve that supplies the motor function to the orbicularis oculi. [32] Although the O’Brien block achieves a more akinetic effect than its counterparts, it carries risks of unilateral facial nerve paresis and nerve root injury. Due to the proximity of the glossopharyngeal and vagus nerves, the internal carotid artery, internal jugular vein, and parotid gland, injection sites closer to the stylomastoid foramen in the modified O’Brien version has been implicated in severe complications such as respiratory and vocal cord paralysis. title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mental Nerve -- Surgical Considerations
content: Surgeons should know the mental nerve sensory territory and its foramen to perform a successful nerve block and prevent complications during surgical procedures. For example, in plastic surgery, the mental nerve is blocked during chin reconstructive interventions. As a result, in mentoplasties and other chin surgeries, the patient may be awake — the nerve block aids in safe and successful surgery with reduced potential complications. The surgeon may also block the mental nerve in the jaw and lower lip surgeries [12] [13] .

title: Local and Regional Anesthesia in Ophthalmology and Ocular Trauma -- Technique or Treatment -- Facial Nerve Blocks
content: The O’Brien block achieves temporary palsy of the orbicularis oculi muscle at the facial nerve trunk. The original version of this block is completed proximally at the level of the mandible near the condyloid process. The modified O’Brien block uses a posterior and inferior position along the most superior palpable aspect of the mandible ramus, approximately 3 cm above the mandible angle. This modified technique provides a highly effective nerve block and lid akinesia at a site closer to the stylomastoid foramen. [12] [32]

content: During parotid gland surgery, branches of the facial nerve may be damaged as the nerve branches off within the parotid gland. After parotid surgery, around 50% develop temporary facial weakness, while 7% end up with permanent facial palsy. [11] Also, an inferior alveolar nerve block during dental procedures may cause facial palsy if the injection goes too deep into the parotid gland. [1]

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Nerve -- Surgical Considerations
content: The lingual nerve can also be the target for a nerve block during certain pathological conditions and procedures. Specifically, a lingual nerve block has been found to be useful for procedures and conditions occurring within the soft tissues of the tongue, including minor infections, traumatic lacerations, and pathological lesions [17] . Benefits of a lingual nerve block instead of an inferior alveolar nerve block include avoiding unnecessary anesthesia of the lip, chin, and buccal soft tissues [17] . This difference is an especially important consideration in patients with mental deficits, children, and the elderly, who present with traumatic lip biting [17] .

title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve and Lingual Nerve Injury -- Etiology
content: Local anesthetic injections are performed prior to most surgical procedures, and both the IAN and LN may be injured during attempts to place blocks around them (see Figure. Conventional Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block). It is very rare, but the needle or the local anesthetic can cause direct or indirect damage to the nerve. The risk is especially increased during the IAN block because the needle travels in close proximity to both the IAN and the LN as they descend medial to the ramus of the mandible. Three theories may explain how nerve damage occurs in these situations. [3]

title: Infraorbital Nerve Block -- Introduction
content: Nerve blocks are effective for providing analgesia in a localized area of the body and offer several advantages over local tissue infiltration. A key benefit of nerve blocks is that they generally require a lower volume of anesthetic to achieve effective analgesia compared to local infiltration, thus reducing distortion of surrounding tissues, which is important in areas such as the face. IIndications for an infraorbital nerve block include wound closure, dental procedures, trigeminal neuralgia, and various procedures on the midface, especially in patients who have a contraindication to general anesthesia. [1] However, nerve blocks are less effective when a wound extends across an area innervated by several nerves.

title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block -- Technique or Treatment -- Conventional Technique
content: The conventional IANB technique is the most commonly used approach. Accurate identification of key anatomical landmarks is essential, including the coronoid process, coronoid notch, anterior and posterior borders of the mandibular ramus, and the sigmoid notch. Additional important landmarks include the pterygomandibular raphe—formed by the buccinator and superior constrictor muscles—and the coronoid notch, with the ideal needle insertion point located between these 2 structures.

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mandibular Nerve -- Clinical Significance
content: Physicians and dentists not achieving complete mandibular nerve anesthesia is a common problem due to many physiological variations. Temporomandibular joint dysfunction (TMD) is caused by a group of conditions that cause pain and joint dysfunction in the jaw joint and muscles that control jaw movement. Many branches of the mandibular nerve are responsible for the control of jaw movement and dysfunction can result in TMD. The auriculotemporal nerve may be damaged during TMJ surgery, which may cause paresthesia of the auricle and the ear region. title: Inferior Alveolar Nerve Block -- Contraindications -- Relative Contraindications
content: Bleeding disorders or anticoagulant therapy: Although not an absolute contraindication, patients with coagulopathies or those receiving anticoagulant medications have an increased risk of hematoma formation, particularly due to the proximity of the pterygoid venous plexus. Severe trismus or limited mandibular opening: Restricted access to the injection site may hinder accurate needle placement, increasing the risk of technical failure or iatrogenic injury. Psychological or behavioral conditions (eg, severe dental anxiety and uncooperative pediatric patients): Such cases may require alternative approaches, including sedation or general anesthesia, to ensure patient safety and cooperation during the procedure. Neurological disorders involving the mandibular nerve: Preexisting conditions such as trigeminal neuralgia or prior nerve injury require careful risk-benefit evaluation and consideration of alternative anesthetic strategies when appropriate.

The block provides sensory anesthesia to the ipsilateral mandibular teeth, the body of the mandible, the lower lip, and part of the buccal and lingual soft tissues. title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mylohyoid Nerve -- Clinical Significance
content: In the clinical setting, the mylohyoid nerve's sensory innervation of the first molar tooth is essential in nerve blocks. The mylohyoid nerve, along with the lingual nerve, is anesthetized during the extraction of the first molar tooth. The inferior alveolar nerve may require anesthetization in procedures involving the chin, lower lip, and gingivae. The nerve block on the inferior alveolar nerve will also block the mylohyoid nerve and the mental nerve, which allows for teeth extractions, suturing, and other procedures in this sensory territory. title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 984
content: Neuromuscular disease (اعتلال عصبي عضلي) is connected to paralysis (شلل), indicating that disorders affecting both nerves and muscles can result in loss of motor function. Muscle disorder (اضطراب العضلات) also has a relationship with paralysis, reinforcing the link between impaired muscle function and the development of paralysis. Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa are related to muscle disorder, suggesting that pathological changes in joint‑related structures can influence muscle health and thereby contribute to paralytic outcomes. More specific conditions, such as lesion in the sciatic nerve (آفة في العصب الوركي) and facial nerve disorder (اضطراب العصب الوجهي), are identified as narrower relationships to paralysis, highlighting that focal nerve injuries or dysfunctions are precise causes of paralytic deficits. Together, these concepts illustrate how a spectrum of neuromuscular, muscular, and peripheral nerve pathologies are interrelated and can culminate in paralysis, underscoring the importance of evaluating both muscle and nerve integrity in patients presenting with motor impairment.

title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
content: Migraine and Headache Syndromes are classified under the broader category of اعتلال عصبي عضلي (neuromuscular disorder). Neuromuscular disorder is associated with شلل (paralysis), indicating that dysfunction of nerves and muscles can lead to loss of motor function. Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa are linked to اضطراب العضلات (muscle disorder), which in turn is also related to شلل, highlighting that pathological changes in musculoskeletal structures may contribute to paralysis. Specific nerve pathologies such as آفة في العصب الوركي (sciatic nerve lesion) and اضطراب العصب الوجهي (facial nerve disorder) have a narrower relationship with شلل, underscoring that localized nerve injuries can directly result in paralysis. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how various headache syndromes, musculoskeletal disorders, and peripheral nerve lesions are interconnected through neuromuscular and muscle dysfunctions that may culminate in paralysis, emphasizing the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of these conditions in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 987
content: اعتلال عصبي عضلي (muscle neuropathy) is linked to شلل (paralysis), indicating that neuropathic involvement of muscle tissue can result in loss of motor function. Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa are associated with اضطراب العضلات (muscle disorder), showing that pathological changes in peri‑articular structures often affect muscle integrity. اضطراب العضلات itself has a relationship with شلل, reinforcing the concept that muscle disorders can progress to or coexist with paralysis. اضطراب العصب الوجهي (facial nerve disorder) includes ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي (upper facial atrophy) as a specific manifestation, and it also has a narrower relationship with شلل, suggesting that facial nerve pathology may contribute to localized or generalized motor weakness. آفة في العصب الوركي (lesion in the sciatic nerve) similarly has a narrower relationship with شلل, highlighting that focal peripheral nerve lesions can directly produce paralysis of the innervated limb. Collectively, these connections illustrate how neuromuscular and musculoskeletal disorders—ranging from muscle neuropathy and tendon‑synovial disease to facial and sciatic nerve lesions—are interrelated pathways that can culminate in paralysis, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and management of both nerve and muscle pathology in patient care.
